```{r}
  playstyle_scale_fill +
  labs(
    title = "A. Game-Specific Wellbeing",
    x = NULL,
    y = "SPANE Balance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1, size = 12),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

# Create boxplot for enjoyment
p_enjoyment <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = player_type, y = enjoyment, fill = player_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = 21) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  playstyle_scale_fill +
  labs(
    title = "B. Enjoyment",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Enjoyment Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1, size = 12),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

# Combine plots
p_wellbeing + p_enjoyment
```

## RQ3. Does Playstyle Moderate the Playtime–Wellbeing Relationship?

```{r}
#| label: moderation-models
#| code-summary: "Fit moderation models (play style × hours interaction)"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Check if models should be refit or loaded from cache
if (eval_models || !model_exists("rmodel_moderation_game") || !model_exists("rmodel_moderation_enjoyment")) {
  # Create interaction models for our two key wellbeing outcomes
  rmodel_moderation_game <- rlmer(
    spane_game_balance ~ player_type * hours + gender + age + (1|country),
    data = pvz_df_clustered
  )

  rmodel_moderation_enjoyment <- rlmer(
    enjoyment ~ player_type * hours + gender + age + (1|country),
    data = pvz_df_clustered
  )
  
  # Save models to cache
  save_model(rmodel_moderation_game, "rmodel_moderation_game")
  save_model(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "rmodel_moderation_enjoyment")
} else {
  # Load cached models
  rmodel_moderation_game <- load_model("rmodel_moderation_game")
  rmodel_moderation_enjoyment <- load_model("rmodel_moderation_enjoyment")
}

# Display moderation models side-by-side using modelsummary
modelsummary(
  list("Wellbeing (SPANE)" = rmodel_moderation_game, "Enjoyment" = rmodel_moderation_enjoyment),
  estimate = "{estimate} ({std.error})",
  statistic = "t = {statistic}",
  gof_omit = "AIC|BIC|RMSE|Log",
  coef_rename = c(
    "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
    "player_typeElite Allround Players" = "Elite Allround Players",
    "player_typeCasual PvP Players" = "Casual PvP Players",
    "hours" = "Hours Played",
    "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours" = "Elite Allround x Hours",
    "player_typeCasual PvP Players:hours" = "Casual PvP x Hours"
  ),
  title = "Moderation Models: Play Style x Hours Interaction",
  notes = "Reference category for play style: Casual PvE Players. Country included as random effect."
)

# Extract coefficients for inline reporting
coefs_mod_game <- coef(summary(rmodel_moderation_game))
coefs_mod_enjoyment <- coef(summary(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment))

r2_mod_game <- compute_r2(rmodel_moderation_game)
r2_mod_enjoyment <- compute_r2(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment)
```

To test whether the playtime–wellbeing association differed across playstyles, we extended the main-effects models by adding player type × hours interaction terms, retaining gender and age as covariates (outcome ~ player_type × hours + gender + age + (1 | country); see Appendix 4). Adding the interaction terms did not meaningfully increase variance explained (wellbeing: marginal *R*² = `r sprintf("%.3f", r2_mod_game["marginal"])`; enjoyment: marginal *R*² = `r sprintf("%.3f", r2_mod_enjoyment["marginal"])`). In the main-effects models, hours played was positively associated with both outcomes (wellbeing: `r fmt_coef(coefs_game, "hours")`; enjoyment: `r fmt_coef(coefs_enjoyment, "hours")`). Each interaction coefficient represents the difference in the playtime slope for a given playstyle relative to Casual PvE Players. None of the interaction terms reached significance: for wellbeing, neither Casual PvP Players (`r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_game, "player_typeCasual PvP Players:hours", "Δb")`) nor Elite Allround Players (`r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_game, "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Δb")`) had a playtime slope that differed from the reference group. The same held for enjoyment (Casual PvP: `r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_enjoyment, "player_typeCasual PvP Players:hours", "Δb")`; Elite Allround: `r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_enjoyment, "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours", "Δb")`). Playstyle differences in intercepts remained in the interaction models, particularly for Casual PvP Players (wellbeing: `r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_game, "player_typeCasual PvP Players")`; enjoyment: `r fmt_coef(coefs_mod_enjoyment, "player_typeCasual PvP Players")`).

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-anova
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Run ANOVAs to test for significant differences between player types
wellbeing_vars <- c("spane_game_balance", "enjoyment")

# Store ANOVA results and models
anova_results <- list()
anova_models <- list()

for (var in wellbeing_vars) {
  formula <- as.formula(paste(var, "~ player_type"))
  anova_model <- aov(formula, data = pvz_df_clustered)
  anova_models[[var]] <- anova_model
  anova_results[[var]] <- broom::tidy(anova_model)
  anova_results[[var]]$variable <- var
  
  # Add effect size
  anova_results[[var]]$eta_squared <- effectsize::eta_squared(anova_model)[["Eta2"]]
  
  # Add residual df
  anova_results[[var]]$df_residual <- summary(anova_model)[[1]]["Residuals", "Df"]
}

# Combine all results
all_anova_results <- bind_rows(anova_results)

# Display results in a nice table
all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type") |>
  select(Variable = variable, df, SS = sumsq, F = statistic, p = p.value, `η²` = eta_squared) |>
  mutate(
    p = case_when(
      p < 0.001 ~ "< .001^†^",
      p < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p, 3)), "^†^"),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p, 3))
    ),
    `η²` = round(`η²`, 3)
  )

# Post-hoc tests for variables with significant differences
posthoc_results <- list()

for (var in wellbeing_vars) {
  if (summary(anova_models[[var]])[[1]]["player_type", "Pr(>F)"] < 0.05) {
    posthoc <- TukeyHSD(anova_models[[var]])
    posthoc_results[[var]] <- broom::tidy(posthoc)
  }
}

# Extract key statistics for inline reporting
wellbeing_anova <- all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type" & variable == "spane_game_balance")
enjoyment_anova <- all_anova_results |> 
  filter(term == "player_type" & variable == "enjoyment")

# Extract post-hoc p-values
wellbeing_posthoc <- posthoc_results[["spane_game_balance"]]
enjoyment_posthoc <- posthoc_results[["enjoyment"]]

# Get specific comparisons (TukeyHSD tidy output uses 'contrast' column)
wb_pvp_pve <- wellbeing_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PvP.*Casual PvE|Casual PvE.*Casual PvP", contrast))
enj_pvp_pve <- enjoyment_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PvP.*Casual PvE|Casual PvE.*Casual PvP", contrast))
enj_pvp_elite <- enjoyment_posthoc |> 
  filter(grepl("Casual PvP.*Elite|Elite.*Casual PvP", contrast))
```

```{r}
#| label: dynamic-results-text
#| echo: false

# Function to format p-value for inline text
format_p_inline <- function(p) {
  if (length(p) == 0 || is.na(p[1])) return("N/A")
  p <- p[1]  # Take first value if vector
  if (p < .001) return("< .001")
  sprintf("= %.3f", p)
}
```

These playstyle differences were corroborated by one-way ANOVAs (wellbeing: *F*(`r wellbeing_anova$df`, `r wellbeing_anova$df_residual`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", wellbeing_anova$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_inline(wellbeing_anova$p.value)`, η² = `r sprintf("%.3f", wellbeing_anova$eta_squared)`; enjoyment: *F*(`r enjoyment_anova$df`, `r enjoyment_anova$df_residual`) = `r sprintf("%.2f", enjoyment_anova$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_inline(enjoyment_anova$p.value)`, η² = `r sprintf("%.3f", enjoyment_anova$eta_squared)`) and Tukey HSD post-hoc tests, which confirmed that Casual PvP Players scored lower than Casual PvE Players on both wellbeing (*p* `r format_p_inline(wb_pvp_pve$adj.p.value)`) and enjoyment (*p* `r format_p_inline(enj_pvp_pve$adj.p.value)`), and lower than Elite Allround Players on enjoyment (*p* `r format_p_inline(enj_pvp_elite$adj.p.value)`).


```{r}
#| label: simple-slopes-functions
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Function to calculate simple slopes for all player types
calculate_all_simple_slopes <- function(model, outcome_name) {
  # For robust mixed models, extract coefficients manually
  coefs <- fixef(model)
  vcov_matrix <- vcov(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Base effect of hours (for reference level)
  hours_effect <- coefs["hours"]
  hours_se <- sqrt(vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"])
  
  # Create results dataframe
  results <- data.frame(
    player_type = player_types[1],  # reference level
    estimate = hours_effect,
    std.error = hours_se,
    statistic = hours_effect / hours_se,
    p.value = 2 * pt(abs(hours_effect / hours_se), 
                    df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - length(coefs), 
                    lower.tail = FALSE),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Calculate for other player types
  for (i in 2:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
    
    if (interaction_term %in% names(coefs)) {
      # Calculate slope
      slope <- hours_effect + coefs[interaction_term]
      
      # Calculate SE using delta method (simplified)
      var_hours <- vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"]
      var_inter <- vcov_matrix[interaction_term, interaction_term]
      cov_hours_inter <- vcov_matrix["hours", interaction_term]
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      # t-statistic and p-value
      t_stat <- slope / slope_se
      p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - length(coefs), lower.tail = FALSE)
      
      # Add to results
      results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
        player_type = pt,
        estimate = slope,
        std.error = slope_se,
        statistic = t_stat,
        p.value = p_val,
        stringsAsFactors = FALSE
      ))
    }
  }
  
  # Add confidence intervals and formatting
  results <- results %>%
    mutate(
      outcome = outcome_name,
      conf.low = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
      conf.high = estimate + 1.96 * std.error,
      significant = p.value < 0.05,
      sig = case_when(
        p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†^",
        p.value < 0.01 ~ "^†^",
        p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^",
        TRUE ~ ""
      )
    )
  
  return(results)
}

# Function to create interaction plots for all player types
create_interaction_plots <- function(model, outcome_name) {
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a grid of values for hours
  hours_range <- range(pvz_df_clustered$hours, na.rm = TRUE)
  hours_grid <- seq(from = hours_range[1], to = hours_range[2], length.out = 100)
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  
  # Create a dataframe to store predictions
  predictions <- data.frame()
  
  # For each player type, calculate predicted values across hours range
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    # Calculate intercept
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"]
      slope <- hours_coef
    } else {
      # Other levels
      intercept <- fixed_effects["(Intercept)"] + fixed_effects[paste0("player_type", pt)]
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
    }
    
    # Calculate predictions
    pred <- intercept + slope * hours_grid
    
    # Add to predictions dataframe
    predictions <- rbind(predictions, data.frame(
      player_type = pt,
      hours = hours_grid,
      predicted = pred
    ))
  }
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(predictions, aes(x = hours, y = predicted, color = player_type)) +
    geom_line(size = 1.2) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = predicted - 1.96 * 0.5, ymax = predicted + 1.96 * 0.5, fill = player_type), alpha = 0.2) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Interaction Plot for", outcome_name),
      x = "Hours Played",
      y = outcome_name,
      color = "Player Type",
      fill = "Player Type"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    playstyle_scale_color +
    playstyle_scale_fill
  
  return(p)
}

# Function to create Johnson-Neyman plot
create_jn_plot <- function(model, moderator = "player_type", predictor = "hours", outcome_name) {
  # For rlmerMod models, we'll use a custom approach
  # This function creates a simplified version of Johnson-Neyman plot
  
  # Extract fixed effects
  fixed_effects <- fixef(model)
  vcov_matrix <- vcov(model)
  
  # Get player types
  player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
  
  # Create a dataframe to store slopes and confidence intervals
  jn_data <- data.frame(
    player_type = player_types,
    slope = numeric(length(player_types)),
    se = numeric(length(player_types)),
    lower_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    upper_ci = numeric(length(player_types)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Get the coefficient for hours (main effect)
  hours_coef <- fixed_effects["hours"]
  hours_se <- sqrt(vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"])
  
  # For each player type, calculate slope and CI
  for (i in 1:length(player_types)) {
    pt <- player_types[i]
    
    if (i == 1) {
      # Reference level
      jn_data$slope[i] <- hours_coef
      jn_data$se[i] <- hours_se
    } else {
      # Other levels
      interaction_term <- paste0("player_type", pt, ":hours")
      slope <- hours_coef + fixed_effects[interaction_term]
      
      # Calculate SE using delta method (simplified)
      var_hours <- vcov_matrix["hours", "hours"]
      var_inter <- vcov_matrix[interaction_term, interaction_term]
      cov_hours_inter <- vcov_matrix["hours", interaction_term]
      slope_se <- sqrt(var_hours + var_inter + 2*cov_hours_inter)
      
      jn_data$slope[i] <- slope
      jn_data$se[i] <- slope_se
    }
    
    # Calculate 95% confidence intervals
    jn_data$lower_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] - 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
    jn_data$upper_ci[i] <- jn_data$slope[i] + 1.96 * jn_data$se[i]
  }
  
  # Determine significance
  jn_data$significant <- (jn_data$lower_ci > 0) | (jn_data$upper_ci < 0)
  
  # Create the plot
  p <- ggplot(jn_data, aes(x = player_type, y = slope, color = significant)) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower_ci, ymax = upper_ci), width = 0.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Conditional Effect of", predictor, "on", outcome_name),
      subtitle = "by Player Type with 95% Confidence Intervals",
      x = "Player Type",
      y = paste("Effect of", predictor),
      color = "Significant"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("gray50", "blue"))
  
  return(p)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Calculate simple slopes for game-specific wellbeing model
wellbeing_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_game, "Game-Specific Wellbeing")

# Create a formatted table for game-specific wellbeing simple slopes
wellbeing_slopes_table <- wellbeing_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(`Player Type` = player_type, b = estimate, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value, `95% CI` = ci)
```

```{r}
#| label: enjoyment-simple-slopes
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Calculate simple slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_slopes <- calculate_all_simple_slopes(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "Intrinsic Enjoyment")

# Create a formatted table for enjoyment simple slopes
enjoyment_slopes_table <- enjoyment_slopes %>%
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    ),
    ci = paste0("[", round(conf.low, 3), ", ", round(conf.high, 3), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(`Player Type` = player_type, b = estimate, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value, `95% CI` = ci)
```

```{r}
#| label: wellbeing-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for game-specific wellbeing
wellbeing_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                        player_type2 = character(),
                        difference = numeric(),
                        std.error = numeric(),
                        statistic = numeric(),
                        p.value = numeric(),
                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

player_types <- levels(pvz_df_clustered$player_type)
for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    wellbeing_pairs <- rbind(wellbeing_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for subjective wellbeing
wellbeing_pairs <- wellbeing_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†^",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "^†^",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^", 
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  rename(`Type 1` = player_type1, `Type 2` = player_type2, Diff = difference, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value)
```

```{r}
#| label: enjoyment-pairwise-comparisons
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Create pairwise comparison of slopes for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                              player_type2 = character(),
                              difference = numeric(),
                              std.error = numeric(),
                              statistic = numeric(),
                              p.value = numeric(),
                              stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    
    # Get slopes and standard errors
    slope1 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    
    # Calculate difference and standard error
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)  # Simplified approach, assumes independence
    
    # Calculate test statistic and p-value
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 10, lower.tail = FALSE)  # Approximate df
    
    # Add to results
    enjoyment_pairs <- rbind(enjoyment_pairs, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1,
      player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff,
      std.error = diff_se,
      statistic = t_stat,
      p.value = p_val,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }
}

# Format and display pairwise comparison table for enjoyment model
enjoyment_pairs <- enjoyment_pairs %>%
  mutate(
    sig = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "^†††^",
      p.value < 0.01 ~ "^††^",
      p.value < 0.05 ~ "^†^", 
      TRUE ~ ""
    ),
    difference = round(difference, 3),
    std.error = round(std.error, 3),
    statistic = round(statistic, 3),
    p.value = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ paste0("< .001", sig),
      p.value < 0.05 ~ paste0(sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3)), sig),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", round(p.value, 3))
    )
  ) |>
  rename(`Type 1` = player_type1, `Type 2` = player_type2, Diff = difference, SE = std.error, t = statistic, p = p.value)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-interaction-plots
#| fig-cap: "Interaction plots showing the relationship between hours played and psychological outcomes across play styles. Left panel shows game-specific wellbeing (SPANE balance score); right panel shows enjoyment. Lines represent linear regression slopes with 95% confidence intervals. Casual PvE Players and Elite Allround Players show positive associations between playtime and outcomes, while Casual PvP Players show no significant relationship."
#| fig-alt: "Two scatter plots side by side with total hours played on the horizontal axis. The left panel plots SPANE balance score (range negative 6 to 6) and the right panel plots enjoyment score (range 1 to 7). In each panel, individual data points are coloured by play style and overlaid with linear regression lines and shaded 95 percent confidence bands. In both panels, Casual PvE Players (blue) and Elite Allround Players (green) show upward-sloping lines indicating positive associations between playtime and outcomes. Casual PvP Players (orange) show a nearly flat line in both panels, indicating no meaningful association. Confidence bands are widest at higher hours values where data are sparser. Most data points cluster below 20 hours of total play."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-summary: "Interaction plots for moderation analysis"
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create scatter plots for total playtime vs. outcomes across player types with APA-style formatting
wellbeing_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = spane_game_balance, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  playstyle_scale_color +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-6, 6, 2)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-6, 6)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "SPANE Score",
    color = "Play Style"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 13),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

enjoyment_plot <- ggplot(pvz_df_clustered, aes(x = hours, y = enjoyment, color = player_type)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, fullrange = TRUE) +
  playstyle_scale_color +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, 1)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(1, 7)) +
  labs(
    x = "Total Hours Played",
    y = "Enjoyment Score",
    color = "Play Style"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 13),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )

ggarrange(wellbeing_plot, enjoyment_plot, ncol = 2, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: simple-slopes-inline-values
#| echo: false

# Extract simple slopes values for inline reporting
# Wellbeing slopes
wb_pve <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PvE Players")
wb_pvp <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PvP Players")
wb_elite <- wellbeing_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Elite Allround Players")

# Enjoyment slopes  
enj_pve <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PvE Players")
enj_pvp <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Casual PvP Players")
enj_elite <- enjoyment_slopes |> filter(player_type == "Elite Allround Players")

# Wellbeing pairwise comparisons (need numeric version before formatting)
wb_pairs_numeric <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                               player_type2 = character(),
                               difference = numeric(),
                               std.error = numeric(),
                               statistic = numeric(),
                               p.value = numeric(),
                               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    slope1 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- wellbeing_slopes$estimate[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- wellbeing_slopes$std.error[wellbeing_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 6, lower.tail = FALSE)
    wb_pairs_numeric <- bind_rows(wb_pairs_numeric, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1, player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff, std.error = diff_se, statistic = t_stat, p.value = p_val
    ))
  }
}

# Enjoyment pairwise comparisons
enj_pairs_numeric <- data.frame(player_type1 = character(),
                                player_type2 = character(),
                                difference = numeric(),
                                std.error = numeric(),
                                statistic = numeric(),
                                p.value = numeric(),
                                stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (i in 1:(length(player_types)-1)) {
  for (j in (i+1):length(player_types)) {
    pt1 <- player_types[i]
    pt2 <- player_types[j]
    slope1 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    slope2 <- enjoyment_slopes$estimate[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    se1 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt1]
    se2 <- enjoyment_slopes$std.error[enjoyment_slopes$player_type == pt2]
    diff <- slope1 - slope2
    diff_se <- sqrt(se1^2 + se2^2)
    t_stat <- diff / diff_se
    p_val <- 2 * pt(abs(t_stat), df = nrow(pvz_df_clustered) - 6, lower.tail = FALSE)
    enj_pairs_numeric <- bind_rows(enj_pairs_numeric, data.frame(
      player_type1 = pt1, player_type2 = pt2,
      difference = diff, std.error = diff_se, statistic = t_stat, p.value = p_val
    ))
  }
}

# Helper function to format p-values for inline text
fmt_p <- function(p) {
  if (p < 0.001) return("< .001")
  sprintf("= %.3f", p)
}
```

To further probe the interaction, simple slopes analyses decomposed the playtime–outcome association within each playstyle (@fig-interaction-plots). Only Elite Allround Players showed a significant positive slope for both wellbeing (b = `r round(wb_elite$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_elite$p.value)`) and enjoyment (b = `r round(enj_elite$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_elite$p.value)`). Slopes for Casual PvE Players (wellbeing: b = `r round(wb_pve$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_pve$p.value)`; enjoyment: b = `r round(enj_pve$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_pve$p.value)`) and Casual PvP Players (wellbeing: b = `r round(wb_pvp$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(wb_pvp$p.value)`; enjoyment: b = `r round(enj_pvp$estimate, 3)`, *p* `r fmt_p(enj_pvp$p.value)`) were not significant. Critically, pairwise comparisons of slopes did not provide evidence that the playtime–outcome association differed between any pair of playstyles (all *p*s > .05), though the present sample may lack the power to detect small slope differences. Taken together, these results indicate that playstyle is associated with different average levels of wellbeing and enjoyment, but the data do not support the conclusion that the playtime–outcome relationship itself varies meaningfully across playstyles.

# Discussion

The objectives of this study were two-fold. First, we aimed to complement the breadth of platform-level playtime research with the depth of granular in-game playstyle analysis. Through telemetry-driven profiling, we aimed to demonstrate how players' in-game behavioural heterogeneity can be modelled within a single game. Second, informed by the Integrated Model of Player Experience, we wanted to explore how players' interaction with the game mechanics and preferences for different in-game modes shape their play experience and subsequent wellbeing, giving us more insight into the conditions under which and for whom video game play might support or undermine wellbeing.

As such, this study addressed three research questions. First, whether distinct playstyles can be identified from in-game behavioural telemetry within a single game. Second, whether players with different playstyles report different levels of wellbeing and enjoyment. Third, whether the association between playtime and wellbeing outcomes differs across playstyles. In short, the findings support the first two but not the third. Three distinct playstyles were identified from behavioural telemetry and labelled to reflect two dimensions that emerged from the data: (1) the game mode of preference (PvE vs. PvP) and (2) their overall performance level (casual vs. elite). These playstyles were associated with meaningful differences in wellbeing and enjoyment, but we found no evidence that they moderated the relationship between playtime and wellbeing or enjoyment. The implications and limitations of these findings will be discussed in detail below.

The participants in the first cluster, named "Casual PvE Players", allocated the majority of their playtime to cooperative PvE modes, achieving moderate performance within that context while showing markedly lower metrics in PvP. This playstyle suggests a preference for story-driven exploration and cooperative play over competitive engagement, paralleling the "Adventurer" type identified by @melhartYourGameplaySays2019 in *Tom Clancy's The Division*, who similarly focused on solo and cooperative mission content rather than competitive play. We hypothesise that these players adopt a more recreation-oriented approach to play, gravitating toward narrative content and cooperative challenges that offer immediate enjoyment without the sustained pressure of competitive evaluation. Within the IMP framework [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014], this pattern reflects a preference for game contexts in which narrative and cooperative mechanics are foregrounded over adversarial mechanics, potentially affording greater perceived autonomy over how to engage with the game's content and a lower threshold for competence satisfaction. Consistent with this interpretation, Casual PvE Players reported the highest game-specific wellbeing and enjoyment of the three groups. This finding is consistent with @melhartYourGameplaySays2019's observation that cooperative and exploratory play features were among the strongest predictors of need satisfaction in *The Division*.

The players in the second cluster, called "Elite Allround Players", formed the largest cluster. They maintained high performance across all gameplay contexts, with kill-to-death ratios and accuracy rates substantially above the sample mean in both PvP and PvE. Rather than specialising in one mode, these players engaged broadly and performed well regardless of context, distinguishing them as the most proficient segment. This playstyle is conceptually consistent with the "Elite" type in @melhartYourGameplaySays2019's analysis of *The Division*, characterised by top-tier progression metrics, and with the high-performing player segments identified by @drachenGunsSwordsData2012 in *Battlefield 2: Bad Company 2*. The breadth of their engagement suggests that mode preference alone does not define this group; rather, it is the consistent pursuit of high performance that characterises their play, consistent with a mastery- or achievement-oriented orientation in which players seek to develop and exercise skill across the full range of available game content. Despite their superior performance, however, Elite Allround Players reported lower wellbeing and enjoyment than Casual PvE Players, though higher enjoyment than Casual PvP Players. This pattern is noteworthy, prior work suggests that competence satisfaction positively predicts wellbeing in gaming contexts [@klimmt2009a; @przybylskiMotivationalModelVideo2010; @tamboriniMediaEnjoymentNeed2011; @ryanHappinessHumanPotentials2001], however if competence alone drove wellbeing, the most skilled players should report the highest wellbeing. Our findings do not support this, suggesting that the context in which competence is exercised matters as well, consistent with the IMP's emphasis on both mechanics and context as jointly shaping player experience [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014]. Potentially, Elite Allround Players' broad engagement across both cooperative and competitive modes may dilute the wellbeing benefits that cooperative PvE environments appear to afford. Furthermore, their high skill may buffer against the competence frustration that competitive environments can impose on less proficient players, though without direct measurement, this remains speculative.

Finally, the players in the third cluster, called "Casual PvP Players", devoted the majority of their time to competitive PvP modes, yet their performance was moderate, falling well below that of Elite Allround Players. Their performance in PvE was similarly modest. This pattern indicates a preference for competitive engagement that is not accompanied by correspondingly high skill, suggesting a casual orientation to play within a competitive context. A comparable playstyle appears in @drachenGunsSwordsData2012's analysis of *Battlefield 2: Bad Company 2*, where a segment of players engaged frequently in competitive modes without achieving high performance metrics, and bears some resemblance to the "Social Dark Zone Player" in @melhartYourGameplaySays2019's *Division* typology, who preferred competitive areas but were distinguished more by their social engagement than their skill. These players were potentially drawn to the multi-player and competitive dynamics of PvP modes rather than to the mastery of competitive mechanics per se. This combination of competitive exposure and moderate skill may create a less optimal play experience. Competitive PvP environments are characterised by direct performance feedback, social comparison, and outcome uncertainty, conditions that could frustrate competence needs when players lack the skill to succeed consistently [@ballouHowVideoGames2025]. Consistent with this reasoning, Casual PvP Players reported the lowest wellbeing and enjoyment of the three groups. The finding that competitive engagement at moderate skill levels is associated with the poorest psychological outcomes aligns with evidence that need frustration in gaming contexts can undermine wellbeing [@ballouHowVideoGames2025], and suggests that the experiential costs of competitive play may not be offset by the social or excitement-related benefits that draw these players to PvP modes.

Lastly, we found no evidence that playstyle moderates the association between playtime and wellbeing or enjoyment. Although Elite Allround Players showed the only significant positive simple slope, this did not translate into a significant difference from the slopes of other groups. These findings align closely with @johannesVideoGamePlay2021, who tested whether self-reported player experiences moderated the playtime-wellbeing association in the same game, *Plants vs. Zombies: Battle for Neighborville*. They found that neither need satisfaction, nor enjoyment, nor extrinsic motivation significantly interacted with playtime in predicting wellbeing, concluding that player experience contributes to wellbeing independently rather than altering how playtime relates to it. Our study extends this result by testing moderation through an entirely different operationalisation, objectively measured behavioural playstyles derived from telemetry, rather than self-reported motivational experiences. That neither subjective player experience nor objective behavioural profile moderates the playtime-wellbeing association in this game strengthens the case that playtime alone is not meaningfully associated with wellbeing for most players [@ballouOpenPlayLongitudinal2025; @johannesVideoGamePlay2021; @vuorreTimeSpentPlaying2022].

It is worth noting, however, that the absence of moderation in our data does not constitute evidence that playtime is wholly irrelevant to wellbeing under all conditions. The playtime-wellbeing relationship is subject to a wide range of time-varying confounds, including physical health, grief, work stress, and leisure availability, that can bias observed associations in either direction [@ballouIllHealthGrief2025]. Our cross-sectional design and limited set of covariates leave open the possibility that such confounds obscure true differential effects. Moreover, our study examines only one game; it remains possible that playstyle-playtime interactions exist in games with greater mechanical diversity, stronger competitive ranking systems, or more pronounced social features. The null moderation finding should therefore be interpreted as specific to the present context rather than as a general claim about the impossibility of such effects.

What the data do show is that the playtime paradigm is incomplete in a critical respect; it misses qualitative differences in play that are associated with meaningful variation in average wellbeing. Playtime treats all minutes of play as equivalent, but players who devote their time to cooperative PvE gameplay report higher wellbeing than those who spend comparable time in competitive PvP modes. This between-group difference in levels, rather than in slopes, is the primary empirical contribution of the present study. In this respect, our results complement rather than contradict platform-level research; playtime may indeed be uninformative as a predictor of wellbeing, but the behavioural composition of that playtime is not.

## Limitations & Future Work

Several limitations constrain the interpretation of these findings and point toward productive directions for future research. First, the study examines a single game with a specific player population recruited by the developer, and we lack evidence that the identified playstyles generalise to other games, genres, or player populations. Unlike personality traits, playstyles may be contextual and fluid, varying with game design and a player's development over time.

Second, while our behavioural telemetry provided comprehensive metrics on core gameplay mechanics and performance, it captured only a subset of potential in-game behaviours. Notably absent were indicators of social interaction, which is an important dimension of multiplayer gaming experiences that the IMP identifies as part of the contextual layer of player experience [@elsonMoreStoriesButtons2014]. In this study, sessions spent in the game's social hub were considered idle time and thus excluded from the playstyle analysis because these sessions generated no mechanics-relevant telemetry on which to cluster. Yet recent work has shown that ostensibly idle or non-interactive periods in games, including socialising between matches and exploring social spaces, can serve experiential functions such as community-building and strategic preparation that shape the broader play experience [@tepponenUnderstandingWaitingVideo2025]. Future work could address this gap by extending the playstyle profiling framework to incorporate social and communicative dimensions of gameplay. Gesture-based digital trace data, such as the 'ping' communication systems common in multiplayer games, have been demonstrated to be related to game context, team dynamics, and performance outcomes [@zhengUnderstandingPlayersGestureBased2023].

Third, the cross-sectional design precludes causal inference. The observed associations between playstyle and wellbeing may reflect selection effects (e.g., players with higher baseline wellbeing may gravitate toward cooperative play) rather than the influence of play behaviour on outcomes. This concern is not merely hypothetical; a wide range of time-varying confounds, from physical health conditions to work stress, plausibly affect both what players do in games and how they feel [@ballouIllHealthGrief2025]. Our design cannot disentangle whether cooperative PvE play promotes wellbeing, whether wellbeing promotes cooperative play, or whether both are driven by unmeasured third variables.

## Conclusion

This study demonstrates that players of the same game engage with it in qualitatively different ways and that these differences are associated with meaningful variation in wellbeing and enjoyment. However, we found no evidence that playstyle moderates the playtime-wellbeing relationship. These findings contribute to the growing body on digital trace research in two respects. First, they provide empirical support for the view that gameplay constitutes heterogeneous exposures with distinct psychological correlates, even within a single title. Second, they demonstrate a practical methodology, telemetry-driven profiling, for incorporating in-game behavioural depth into wellbeing research. At the same time, the null moderation results caution against overclaiming; the present data show that what players do is associated with how they feel, but not that different styles of play change how playtime itself relates to wellbeing. As richer forms of in-game data become available through industry-academic partnerships, researchers should dig deeper and continue to develop more granular, theory-driven approaches to understanding player experience and its relationship to psychological outcomes.

## Acknowledgements

Claude (Sonnet/Opus 4.6; Anthropic) was used to assist with code development and minor text edits during the preparation of this manuscript. All AI-assisted contributions were reviewed, verified, and, where necessary, further revised by members of the author team, who maintain full responsibility for the final content.

## Author contributions
Conceptualization: TH, AKP;
Data curation: TH, MV;
Formal analysis: TH, MV;
Funding acquisition: AKP;
Investigation: TH;
Methodology: TH, MV;
Project administration: AKP;
Supervision: MV, AKP;
Visualization: TH;
Writing – original draft: TH;
Writing – review & editing: TH, MV, AKP


## Data Availability

All data, materials, and code related to the dataset and this manuscript are available under {{< meta data-license >}} at <https://github.com/Thomhak/playstyle-article.git>.

## Funding

[funding]

## Conflicts of Interest

The authors declare no competing financial, intellectual, or institutional interests relevant to this research. The disclosures below are provided in the interest of full transparency.
No authors have received funding or compensation from the tech companies or other industries connected to the work presented here.
MV’s work is or has been funded by the European Research Council, the Economic and Social Research Council, Tilburg University, and the University of Oxford’s John Fell Fund. MV has provided in-kind consultations and served as a nonpaid panel member for Meta and K-Games. AKP has served in advisory or expert roles for the Sync Digital Wellbeing Program (2022–2024), the Google Expert Advisory on Youth and Technology (2025), the OpenAI Expert Council on Wellbeing and AI (2025), and UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology–funded research on children's smartphone and social media use (University of Cambridge, 2025). He is a member of the UK Department for Culture, Media & Sport College of Experts. Any industry fees are directly donated to charity, and his research is conducted in accordance with University of Oxford academic integrity policies.

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=typst}
#show: appendix.with()
```

# Appendix {.appendix}

## Appendix 1: Cluster Tendency Assessment

```{r}
#| label: appendix_cluster_tendency
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 10
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig.cap: "Cluster tendency assessment comparing player data (left column) to random data (right column). Top row: Visual Assessment of Cluster Tendency (VAT) ordered dissimilarity matrices. Bottom row: PCA visualizations. The player data shows clear clustering structure (dark blocks in VAT, visible groupings in PCA) while random data shows no such patterns."
#| fig.alt: "Two-by-two grid of four panels. Top-left (A, VAT Player Data): a heatmap of the reordered distance matrix showing three distinct dark-coloured square blocks along the diagonal, indicating three natural groups. Top-right (B, VAT Random Data): a similar heatmap for uniformly random data showing no block structure, with distances distributed evenly as a smooth gradient. Bottom-left (C, PCA Player Data): a two-dimensional scatter plot of the first two principal components with points coloured by cluster assignment, revealing three visually separable groups. Bottom-right (D, PCA Random Data): the same PCA projection for random data showing a single diffuse cloud with no discernible groupings."

# Combine all four plots into a 2x2 grid
grid.arrange(
  dist_plot_real + labs(title = "A: VAT - Player Data"),
  dist_plot_random + labs(title = "B: VAT - Random Data"),
  pca_plot_real + labs(title = "C: PCA - Player Data"),
  pca_plot_random + labs(title = "D: PCA - Random Data"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2
)
```

## Appendix 2: Cluster Validation Plots

```{r}
#| label: appendix_cluster_validation_plots
#| fig.width: 6
#| fig.height: 4
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig.cap: "Cluster validation using the Calinski-Harabasz Index across k = 2 to 10."
#| fig.alt: "Line chart with number of clusters k (2 through 10) on the horizontal axis and the Calinski-Harabasz Index on the vertical axis. The line peaks sharply at k equals 3, then declines steadily as k increases, indicating that three clusters provide the best ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster variance for this dataset."

# Display CH-index plot in appendix
p_pam_ch
```

The plot above shows the validation method used to determine the optimal number of clusters. The Calinski-Harabasz Index evaluates cluster validity based on the ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster variance, with higher values indicating better separation between clusters. The index indicated that k = 3 was the optimal number of clusters for our player data.

## Appendix 3: Robust Mixed-Model Coefficients

```{r}
#| label: appendix_model_coefficients
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

fmt_p_table <- function(p) {
  case_when(
    is.na(p) ~ "NA",
    p < 0.001 ~ "< .001",
    TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", p)
  )
}

tidy_rlmer_table <- function(model, outcome, model_name) {
  as.data.frame(coef(summary(model))) %>%
    rownames_to_column("term") %>%
    as_tibble() %>%
    rename(
      estimate = Estimate,
      std_error = `Std. Error`,
      t_value = `t value`
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      outcome = outcome,
      model = model_name,
      p_approx = 2 * pnorm(-abs(t_value))
    )
}

term_map <- c(
  "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
  "player_typeCasual PvP Players" = "Casual PvP vs Casual PvE",
  "player_typeElite Allround Players" = "Elite Allround vs Casual PvE",
  "hours" = "Hours played",
  "genderMale" = "Gender: Male (vs Female)",
  "genderOther" = "Gender: Other (vs Female)",
  "genderPrefer not to say" = "Gender: Undisclosed (vs Female)",
  "age" = "Age",
  "player_typeCasual PvP Players:hours" = "Casual PvP x Hours",
  "player_typeElite Allround Players:hours" = "Elite Allround x Hours"
)

bind_rows(
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_game, "Wellbeing", "Main effects"),
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_enjoyment, "Enjoyment", "Main effects"),
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_moderation_game, "Wellbeing", "Moderation"),
  tidy_rlmer_table(rmodel_moderation_enjoyment, "Enjoyment", "Moderation")
) %>%
  mutate(term = recode(term, !!!term_map, .default = term)) %>%
  filter(term != "Intercept") %>%
  transmute(
    Model = model,
    Outcome = outcome,
    Term = term,
    b = round(estimate, 3),
    SE = round(std_error, 3),
    t = round(t_value, 2),
    p = fmt_p_table(p_approx)
  ) %>%
  tt(caption = "Fixed-effect coefficients from robust linear mixed-effects models (rlmer) with random intercepts for country. Approximate p-values derived from normal-approximation of t-statistics.") %>%
  format_tt(digits = 3)
```

This table presents the full coefficient estimates for all four robust mixed-effects models. ANOVA omnibus tests, Tukey HSD post-hoc comparisons, simple slopes, and pairwise slope-difference tests are reported in the main text.
